text: This is a case presentation from the Cleveland Clinic on a robotic intersphincter resection for low rectal neuroendocrine tumor. Fifty-year-old male with past surgical history of nasal surgery, otherwise healthy, who was recently diagnosed with neuroendocrine tumor of the distal rectum was presented to the clinic for further surgical evaluation.

Excisional biopsy at the outside hospital showed low-grade neuroendocrine tumor with positive margins. Pathologic findings revealed that there are infiltrating cords of tumor cells in the background of dense fibrous stroma and diffuse strong cytoplasmic expression for synaptophysin.

Colonoscopy performed at our clinic showed a scar tissue from the previous biopsy site. and biopsy results consistent with granulation tissue. Puraoperative pelvic MRI demonstrated enhancing parietal lymph node.
The patient is positioned in the modified lithotomy position for a robotic intersphincter resection of a neuroendocrine tumor located on top of the sphincter muscle.

Following diagnostic exploration confirming no metastatic disease, additional trocars are placed for the robotic procedure. The robot is docked from the left side of the patient, with robotic arms positioned as the patient is placed in the right side down and Trendelenburg position.
The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection, identifying, ligating, and dividing the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon detaches the lateral attachments and mobilizes the splenic flexure. The dissection progresses into the rectum, revealing the anatomical structures involved.

The surgeon uses the robotic third arm, repositioned from the right lower quadrant to the left lower quadrant, to perform a total mesorectal excision down to the pelvic floor. The dissection is carried around the puborectalis muscles, with confirmation of the step through digital examination.

Following the completion of the abdominal dissection, the surgeon shifts focus to the pelvic approach. Anal retraction sutures are placed, and a circumferential mucosal incision is made at the dentate line.
After completing the interspectral dissection, the surgeon places a wound protector and pulls the bowel through it. A coloanal anastomosis is then created using two ovary sutures.

The postoperative recovery was uneventful and patient was discharged to home on postoperative day six. The final pathology revealed The final pathology demonstrated well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor with one metastatic lymph node.
All margins. In conclusion, this case demonstrates the feasibility of the robotic intersphincter resection in a patient with narrow and difficult pelvis.
